required.title: [Map] USGS 7.5-minute topographic maps of your local area

suggested.title: [Book]
suggested.author: Bob Burns and Mike Burns (Mountaineers Books)

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: Land Navigation with a Map and Compass (US Army Training)

competence_markers.description: You can shoot a bearing, follow a bearing, and account for magnetic declination using a baseplate compass
You can read a topographic map: identify ridgelines, valleys, peaks, saddles, and cliffs from contour lines
You can calculate total elevation gain on a planned route
You can navigate by dead reckoning (compass + pace count) across at least 500 meters to a target within 50 meters of accuracy
You can determine your position on a map using triangulation (at least two bearings)
You can navigate using terrain association — matching observed terrain to map features while moving
You can explain and use handrailing and attack points
You have completed at least one orienteering course of 2+ kilometers with 5+ checkpoints using only map and compass
You can navigate at night using Polaris with sufficient accuracy to maintain a general heading

narration_prompts.detail: What is it? Why does it matter? What happens if you ignore it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a ridgeline and a valley on a topographic map.
narration_prompts.detail: How do the contour lines differ? How would you recognize each in the field?

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through a dead reckoning exercise.
narration_prompts.detail: How do you combine compass bearing and pace count to reach a target you cannot see?

narration_prompts.prompt: How do you determine your position using triangulation?
narration_prompts.detail: What do you need to see? What calculations do you make?
How is it different from just following a compass bearing?

narration_prompts.prompt: You are navigating in thick fog. You cannot see more than 30 meters. You need to reach a trail junction 1.5 kilometers away. Walk through your plan.

cross_connections.description: Compass bearings are angles. Scale conversion is proportional reasoning. Pace counting is applied multiplication. Triangulation is geometry. This packet is applied mathematics in the field.
Topographic maps represent the same landforms — watersheds, ridgelines, floodplains — that 1.2.1 studies. The student who reads a topo map is doing geography with their feet.
Land navigation under load, at speed, with time pressure is where tactical fitness and navigation merge. Orienteering at competitive intensity is a physical and cognitive workout.
Route planning requires integrating navigation with weather assessment. The go/no-go decision is a navigation question as much as a weather question.

Navigating in poor visibility, under time pressure, or when you realize you are off course tests emotional regulation. The student who stays calm and works the problem instead of panicking has IC3 competence.
Both the bow drill and compass navigation require sustained, deliberate practice to develop reliable skill. The patience formed in one transfers to the other.

content.why_this_matters:
You can find north using the sun and stars. You can read a simple map and navigate by landmarks. Those skills put you ahead of most people. But they are not precise enough to get you from point A to point B across unfamiliar terrain when the trail disappears, when fog reduces visibility to fifty yards, or when darkness falls and you still have two miles to cover.

This packet gives you precision. You will learn to use a compass — not as a novelty but as a genuine navigation tool that works in any weather, any terrain, any time of day. You will learn to read topographic maps — maps that show you not just what is on the ground, but the shape of the ground itself: where ridges rise, where valleys drop, where streams flow, and how steep the slopes are. You will learn dead reckoning — measuring your travel by direction and distance without visible landmarks. And you will learn triangulation — determining exactly where you are on a map by taking bearings to features you can identify.

These are the skills that let a person walk confidently into unfamiliar terrain and walk out again. They are the skills that search-and-rescue teams use. They are the skills that every military force teaches. And they are the skills that, when practiced through orienteering, become as natural as walking.
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Baseplate Compass

The compass is the most reliable navigation instrument available to you. It works in fog, in darkness, in rain, in any terrain. It requires no batteries, no satellite signal, no cell coverage. It is also one of the most commonly misused tools in outdoor recreation — many people own a compass and cannot actually navigate with one.

**Components of a baseplate compass:**

- **Baseplate** — the flat, transparent base. The long edge is used for drawing lines on the map.
- **Direction-of-travel arrow** — printed on the baseplate. This points the way you walk.
- **Rotating housing** — the circular dial with degree markings (0-360). You turn this to set your bearing.
- **Magnetic needle** — the red end points to magnetic north. Always. This is the compass's only job.
- **Orienting arrow** — printed on the bottom of the housing. You rotate the housing until the magnetic needle sits inside this arrow ("red in the shed").
- **Orienting lines** — parallel lines on the housing floor, used to align the compass with map grid lines.
- **Declination adjustment** — a small screw or mechanism that offsets the orienting arrow to account for the difference between magnetic north and true north.

**Shooting a bearing (direction to a visible target):**

1. Hold the compass flat at waist height
2. Point the direction-of-travel arrow at your target
3. Rotate the housing until the magnetic needle sits inside the orienting arrow (red in the shed)
4. Read the number at the direction-of-travel arrow. That is your bearing in degrees.

**Following a bearing (walking in a specific direction):**

1. Set the desired bearing by rotating the housing until the correct degree number is at the direction-of-travel arrow
2. Hold the compass flat and turn your entire body until the magnetic needle sits inside the orienting arrow
3. The direction-of-travel arrow now points the way you need to walk
4. Pick a visible landmark in that direction — a tree, a rock, a distant feature — and walk to it
5. At the landmark, repeat: check compass, find next landmark, walk to it. This "leapfrogging" technique prevents drift.

### Part 2: Magnetic Declination

Magnetic north and true north are not the same place. The magnetic North Pole is in northern Canada, while true north — the top of the Earth's axis — is the geographic North Pole. The angle between them, from your location, is called **magnetic declination**.

Declination varies by location. In the eastern United States, magnetic north is west of true north (declination is negative/west). In the western United States, magnetic north is east of true north (declination is positive/east). In some locations, declination is zero.

**Why it matters:** If your declination is 15 degrees west and you do not account for it, you will be 15 degrees off course — which over 1 kilometer translates to roughly 260 meters of error. Over longer distances, this error compounds. Uncorrected declination is one of the most common causes of navigational error.

**How to correct:** The simplest method is to adjust your compass's built-in declination adjustment so the compass reads true north directly. Your topographic map's margin notes state the declination for that area (and the date, since declination changes slowly over time). Set it once and forget it — until you move to a different region.

### Part 3: Topographic Maps

A topographic map shows the three-dimensional shape of the terrain on a two-dimensional sheet of paper using **contour lines** — lines that connect points of equal elevation.

**Reading contour lines:**

- Every point on a single contour line is the same elevation above sea level
- The **contour interval** (stated in the map's legend) tells you the elevation change between adjacent lines — commonly 40 feet on USGS 7.5-minute maps
- **Closely spaced contours** = steep terrain. **Widely spaced contours** = gentle terrain. **No contours** = flat.
- **Index contours** — every fifth contour line is thicker and labeled with its elevation. Use these to count elevation quickly.

**Terrain features you must recognize:**

- **Ridgeline** — contours forming a "V" or "U" pointing from higher to lower elevation. The ridge runs along the high ground between the V's.
- **Valley/drainage** — contours forming a "V" pointing uphill (toward higher elevation). Water flows down the center of the V.
- **Peak/summit** — concentric closed contour circles, smallest at the top
- **Saddle** — a low point between two peaks, shaped like an hourglass in the contours
- **Cliff** — contours so close they merge or nearly merge. Extremely steep terrain.
- **Depression** — closed contour circles with tick marks pointing inward (downhill). A hole in the ground.

**Calculating elevation gain:** Count the contour lines you will cross on your planned route and multiply by the contour interval. A route that crosses 15 contour lines on a 40-foot-interval map gains 600 feet of elevation.

### Part 4: Dead Reckoning and Pace Counting

Dead reckoning is navigating by compass bearing and measured distance when you cannot see your destination. It is essential in low visibility — fog, dense forest, darkness — and on featureless terrain where landmarks are scarce.

**Pace counting:**

Your pace count is the number of double-steps (every time your left foot hits the ground) it takes you to cover 100 meters. This varies by person and terrain.

**Calibrating your pace count:**

1. Measure a 100-meter course on flat ground (use a tape measure or known distance)
2. Walk it at a normal pace, counting every time your left foot strikes
3. Repeat three times and average the results
4. That is your flat-terrain pace count
5. Now calibrate for uphill (pace count increases), downhill (slightly increases), and dense vegetation (significantly increases)

**Using pace count with compass:**

If you need to walk 300 meters on a bearing of 045 degrees:
1. Set 045 on your compass
2. Orient yourself
3. Walk, counting double-steps
4. When you reach 3x your 100-meter pace count, you have covered approximately 300 meters

**Error accumulates.** Over short distances, dead reckoning is reasonably accurate. Over long distances, small errors in bearing and pace count compound. To manage this, use dead reckoning for short legs between visible features whenever possible, rather than one long leg.

### Part 5: Triangulation and Resection

When you can see identifiable landmarks that also appear on your map, you can determine exactly where you are.

**Resection (finding your position):**

1. Identify two (preferably three) landmarks that you can see AND find on your map — a mountain peak, a church steeple, a distinct bend in a river
2. Shoot a compass bearing to each landmark
3. Convert each bearing to a **back-bearing** (add 180 degrees if the bearing is less than 180; subtract 180 if greater)
4. On the map, draw a line from each landmark along the back-bearing
5. Where the lines intersect is your position

Three bearings are better than two because the intersection of two lines gives one point, but three lines form a small triangle — the smaller the triangle, the more accurate your position.

### Part 6: Terrain Association

Terrain association is the art of navigating by continuously matching what you see in the real world to what the map shows. It is the highest-reliability method in most conditions because it does not depend on precise instrument readings — it depends on observation.

**The practice:** As you move, constantly compare the terrain around you to the map. "The map shows a ridgeline on my left that curves southeast, and I can see that ridgeline. The map shows a creek crossing ahead, and I can hear water. The map shows the slope steepening — and it is."

Terrain association works best when combined with other methods. Use your compass for direction. Use pace count for distance. Use the terrain to confirm both.

### Part 7: Handrailing and Attack Points

**Handrailing:** Using a linear terrain feature — a ridgeline, a stream, a road, a fence line, a power line — as a guide. Instead of navigating through open terrain on a bearing, you follow the linear feature. This is especially useful when your target is near a linear feature — "follow the creek upstream until you reach the waterfall."

**Attack points:** Navigate first to a large, easily found feature near your target. Then make a short, precise move from the attack point to the actual target. For example: your destination is a small campsite. The campsite is 200 meters south of a prominent trail junction. Navigate to the trail junction (easy to find) and then walk 200 meters south (short, precise).

### Part 8: Celestial Navigation — Greater Precision

You found Polaris in Stretching Branches. Now you use it with more precision.

**Polaris gives you true north.** This is important because your compass gives you magnetic north. At night, you can check your compass declination against Polaris — if your declination-adjusted compass points at Polaris, your adjustment is correct.

**The moon for approximate direction:**

- When the moon rises before midnight, the bright side faces roughly east
- When the moon rises after midnight, the bright side faces roughly west
- A first-quarter moon is highest in the sky at sunset and is roughly due south at that moment (Northern Hemisphere)

These are approximations — useful when you need a rough check without instruments.

**The shadow-stick method with greater precision:** The east-west line from the shadow stick (Stretching Branches) can now be used with a map. Align the east-west line with the map's east-west grid lines to orient the map without a compass.

### Part 9: Orienteering — Navigation as Practice

Orienteering is competitive navigation: you receive a topographic map with marked checkpoints, and you navigate from checkpoint to checkpoint as quickly and accurately as possible. It is the most effective training for all the skills in this packet because:

- Mistakes are immediately visible (you reach the wrong location or take too long)
- Feedback is instant (every course reveals your strengths and weaknesses)
- It integrates all skills: map reading, compass, terrain association, route choice, pace counting
- It scales from beginner courses (short distances, obvious features) to expert courses (long distances, subtle terrain, navigation under pressure)

**If formal orienteering events are not available in your area,** your mentor creates courses using local topographic maps: mark 5-8 waypoints at identifiable features (trail junctions, stream confluences, hilltops, distinct bends), and navigate to each in sequence.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Compass Use

**Developing well:** The student shoots bearings within 3-5 degrees of the correct value. They set and follow bearings smoothly, using the leapfrog technique to maintain accuracy. Declination is set correctly and the student can explain why. They hold the compass level without being reminded.

**Developing slowly:** Bearings are consistently off by more than 10 degrees. The student does not use the leapfrog technique (they stare at the compass while walking, which causes drift). Declination is not set or not understood.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student holding the compass flat and at waist level? (Tilting causes the needle to stick.) Are they turning their whole body to orient, or just turning the compass? (Only whole-body rotation works.) Can they explain what declination is and why it matters? (If not, the concept needs re-teaching.)

### 2. Topographic Map Reading

**Developing well:** The student can look at a section of topo map and describe the terrain it represents: "This is a steep ridge running north-south with a creek in the valley on the east side. The slope is gradual on the west but drops off steeply on the east." They can identify the major terrain features by name (ridge, valley, saddle, peak).

**Developing slowly:** The student sees contour lines as abstract patterns rather than terrain. They cannot translate the map into a mental picture of what the landscape looks like. They confuse ridges and valleys (a common error — the V-points-uphill rule for valleys is counterintuitive).

**Diagnostic questions:** Take the student to a real hilltop and look at the topo map together. Point to the contour lines representing where you are standing and ask them to trace the slope downward. Then walk downhill and show them how the contour spacing corresponds to steepness. The connection between paper and reality often clicks when demonstrated in person.

### 3. Dead Reckoning

**Developing well:** On a 500-meter dead reckoning course, the student arrives within 50 meters of the target. They count paces automatically. They use the leapfrog method for bearing. They can describe their error sources: "I think I drifted right because I was avoiding that thicket."

**Developing slowly:** The student arrives more than 100 meters from the target. They lose count of their paces. They do not use visible landmarks to maintain bearing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the pace count calibrated correctly? (Verify on the measured course.) Is the student actually counting, or estimating? (Have them count aloud for one leg.) Is the terrain appropriate for the skill level? (Start with open terrain; dense forest and steep slopes amplify error.)

### 4. Triangulation

**Developing well:** The student identifies landmarks on the map, shoots accurate bearings, calculates back-bearings correctly, and plots a position that is within 100-200 meters of their actual position. They understand why three bearings are better than two.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot identify real-world features on the map (the fundamental skill of terrain association). Back-bearing calculation is consistently wrong. Lines on the map do not intersect near each other (indicating large bearing errors).

**Diagnostic questions:** Start with very obvious features — a distinct mountain peak, a church steeple, a radio tower. If the student cannot find these on the map, the issue is map-to-terrain matching, not triangulation math. Build the matching skill first.

### 5. Orienteering Course

**Developing well:** The student completes a 2+ kilometer course with 5+ checkpoints, finding all or nearly all checkpoints. Route choices are deliberate (the student can explain why they chose to go over the ridge versus around it). Time is reasonable for the difficulty.

**Developing slowly:** The student misses multiple checkpoints. They wander without a clear plan. They do not orient their map before leaving each checkpoint. Route choices are random rather than deliberate.

**Diagnostic questions:** After the course, debrief each leg. Where did the student make route choices? Were they conscious choices or defaults? Where did navigation break down? What would they do differently? The debrief is as valuable as the course itself.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Correcting Navigation Errors in Real Time
When you see the student heading off course, the temptation is to say "you're going the wrong way." Resist this unless there is a safety issue. Let them navigate until they realize the error themselves. The self-correction process — recognizing the error, diagnosing the cause, adjusting — is the most valuable learning in navigation. If they reach the wrong location, ask: "Where do you think you are? What does the map say should be here?"

### Using GPS as Primary Verification
If you check the student's position with GPS after every exercise, the student learns to depend on the GPS correction rather than developing internal confidence. Use GPS sparingly — perhaps once per session as a final check, not as ongoing feedback.

### Flat Terrain Only
Navigation on flat, featureless terrain is the hardest type (no terrain features to confirm position). Navigation in varied terrain with ridges, streams, and distinct features is actually easier and more educational. Do not mistake flat terrain for "beginner terrain" — it is advanced.

### Skipping Orienteering
If no formal events are available, the temptation is to skip this checkpoint. Do not. Create your own courses. They do not need to be perfect — even a simple course with 5 checkpoints in a local park provides valuable integrated practice.
